column6: description
Product security checks must inspect only the configured product repository and must not scan unrelated directories.
Secret-like values, private keys, credentials, tokens, or private messages must not be committed, printed, or logged.
Environment files should be ignored or documented; warnings must not hide high-confidence secret exposure.
External integrations require explicit service, data, OAuth, token, webhook, logging, rate-limit, sandbox, and rollback confirmation before implementation.
Dependency, authentication, API, storage, deletion, or logging changes require security-oriented review.
Product work must preserve existing Git sync, CI, workflow-pair, and repository-boundary gates.
Product security must keep advisory warnings separate from high-confidence blocking failures.
Product security output may show categories and file references, but must not print secret values.

Parent document-sync enforcement must not expand product registry paths, scan child repositories, invoke child tests, use network services, or treat external evidence as a parent synchronization substitute.
Verification receipts must not contain raw output, environment dumps, secrets, credentials, repository URLs, or host-specific absolute paths.
Composed verification must use validated argv, bounded output, configured timeouts and locks, and process-group cancellation without granting external-repository or destructive authority.
Dashboard build and browser success may be composed only within one run when revision, policy, command, source, output, and test inventory digests match exactly.

Dashboard build caches and other mutable tool state must remain outside immutable source and configuration inventories, with the shared configuration helper failing closed before clean-run verification begins.
Distributed CI results may satisfy a final gate only through exact semantic ownership and a proof bound to the current workflow graph, policy, catalog, revision, and provider results.
A present target-local instruction memory must validate and must never be treated as absent, merged with the parent fallback, or overwritten from the parent.
Applicable AGENTS.MD rules are invariant authority; local and parent instruction memories are procedural only and cannot weaken those invariants.

Product fallback is limited to the explicitly selected parent-managed Git top-level; lesson contexts, standalone, reconnecting, repair-required, mismatched, nested, or unselected targets are rejected or not applicable.
Instruction and policy files must be bounded regular non-symlink UTF-8 files with stable identity, safe configured paths, no NUL, supported versions, and unique A-F headings.
Resolver output contains safe relative source identity and digests only; it must not emit raw instruction content, secrets, credentials, repository URLs, or host-specific absolute paths.
The parent A-F fallback does not create task scope, bypass Settings, authorize destructive or credential work, merge failed CI, expand scope, or replace release proof.
Git, provider, Agent launch, filesystem, adapter, runtime, dependency, and resource effects require a fresh tagged decision through the common gateway.
Auto, Manual, Inherit, custom CLI/API, and local runtime selections share one certification, endpoint, secret-reference, authority, attestation, and budget floor.
Parent CI and next-workflow tests use isolated fixtures and never traverse, test, mutate, or accept evidence directly from a registered child repository.
A post-exec sandbox error may preserve Git evidence only when the completed child status is exactly zero and stdout is a byte buffer; nonzero, missing, or ambiguous results fail closed.

Task Agent CLI execution requires owner-trusted executable hashes, Linux user/mount/network/PID isolation, Bubblewrap, private input/output roots, exact runtime budgets, and store-bound lifecycle authority; live provider probes keep the host root read-only and bind their pinned executable below a private writable tmpfs, while missing prerequisites stop with non-installing guidance and never permit an unisolated fallback.
A CLI response remains an unaccepted candidate until durable launch/admission/runtime provenance and independent Lead, Orchestrator, and Validator records close the exact lineage.

Production commands must enter through the externally installed owner-trusted launcher, which verifies its pinned shell parent, exact argument tail, repository identity, executable hashes, and activated release before repository code runs.

Production Activation requires an immutable Git candidate plus independently signed source receipts, release evidence, ordered transition proofs, recovery/rollback/archive/outbox closure, PR CI, main CI, and exact local/remote synchronization.

A different immutable candidate may replace an enforced release only through a fresh complete shadow-to-enforced chain; same-candidate rewind, rolled-back restart, incomplete-cycle enforcement, prior-record deletion, and fallback to an older enforced candidate are blocked.
Public task input cannot downgrade admitted rigor or reveal private topology; L1 runs exactly one Orchestrator and L2-L5 require the admitted Orchestrator, Lead, and Task Agent hierarchy.

Private complete non-executed provider descriptors may make bootstrap, installed-launcher integrity, injected discovery, and development-selection tests portable when CI lacks Codex, but they must never become provider certification, Product inheritance, provider execution, Production launch evidence, or Activation authority; real containment is never fixture-substituted, and its absence skips all positive trust/launcher cases and keeps Production blocked.

The external Controller installer and every Owner action must obtain the logical repository and per-worktree checkout instance from the checkout-identity owner; runtime lookup cannot create identity, the wrapper pins the verified Controller base, and missing, invalid, substituted, or redirected identity blocks before mutation.
